blocks:
Ayahs

لَآ أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (١)
I swear by the Day of Resurrection! (1)
وَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلنَّفْسِ ٱللَّوَّامَةِ (٢)
And I swear by the self-critical soul! (2)
أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَلَّن نَّجْمَعَ عِظَامَهُۥ (٣)
Does man suppose that We will not put together his bones [at resurrection]? (3)
بَلَىٰ قَـٰدِرِينَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّسَوِّىَ بَنَانَهُۥ (٤)
Yes indeed, We are able to [re]shape [even] his fingertips! (4)
بَلْ يُرِيدُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمَامَهُۥ (٥)
Indeed, man desires to go on living viciously. (5)
يَسْـَٔلُ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (٦)
He asks, ‘When will be this “day of resurrection”?!’ (6)
فَإِذَا بَرِقَ ٱلْبَصَرُ (٧)
But when the eyes are dazzled, (7)
وَخَسَفَ ٱلْقَمَرُ (٨)
the moon is eclipsed, (8)
وَجُمِعَ ٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ (٩)
and the sun and the moon are brought together, (9)
يَقُولُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَيْنَ ٱلْمَفَرُّ (١٠)
that day man will say, ‘Where is the escape [from this day]?’ (10)
كَلَّا لَا وَزَرَ (١١)
No indeed! There will be no refuge! (11)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمُسْتَقَرُّ (١٢)
That day the [final] goal will be toward your Lord. (12)
يُنَبَّؤُا۟ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بِمَا قَدَّمَ وَأَخَّرَ (١٣)
That day man will be informed about what [works] he had sent ahead [to the scene of judgement] and [the legacy that he had] left behind. (13)
بَلِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ بَصِيرَةٌ (١٤)
Indeed, man is a witness to himself, (14)
وَلَوْ أَلْقَىٰ مَعَاذِيرَهُۥ (١٥)
though he should offer excuses [to justify his failings]. (15)

(Commentary)

The discourse of this Surah revolves around the Great Resurrection (القيامة الكبرى). It first announces the occurrence of the Day of Resurrection, then describes it sometimes by its portents (أشراط) and at other times by a summary of what will befall man. It further informs that the path leading toward it begins from the day of death, and concludes by arguing for the possibility of restoration (الإعادة) through the power of initiation (الابتداء).

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: لا أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ — "I swear by the Day of Resurrection!" (75:1). This is an oath by the Day of Resurrection, whether the phrase "I do not swear" (لا أُقْسِمُ) is considered a formula of oath, or the "no" (لا) is considered redundant (زائدة) or negating (نافية), according to various opinions.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا أُقْسِمُ بِالنَّفْسِ اللَّوَّامَةِ — "And I swear by the self-critical soul!" (75:2). This is a second oath, as necessitated by the context and the parallelism of the phrasing. No weight should be given to the view that it is a negation of the oath rather than an oath itself—meaning: "I swear by the Day of Resurrection, but I do not swear by the self-critical soul."

What is meant by the self-critical soul (النفس اللوامة) is the soul of the believer (المؤمن) which reproaches him in this world for disobedience and for being sluggish in obedience, and which will benefit him on the Day of Resurrection.

It has also been said that it refers to the human soul in general, encompassing both the righteous believer and the profligate disbeliever, for it will reproach the human being on the Day of Resurrection. As for the disbeliever, it will reproach him for his disbelief and profligacy; as for the believer, it will reproach him for his deficiency in obedience and for not increasing his good deeds.

It has further been said that it refers to the soul of the disbeliever, which will reproach him on the Day of Resurrection for the disbelief and disobedience he sent forward. The Exalted said: وَأَسَرُّوا النَّدامَةَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا الْعَذابَ — "They will hide their remorse when they sight the punishment" (10:54).

Each of these views has a basis.

The response to the oath (جواب القسم) is omitted, but indicated by the subsequent verses; the implied meaning is: "You shall surely be resurrected." It was omitted to signify the magnitude (تفخيم) of that Day and the gravity of its affair. The Exalted said: ثَقُلَتْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً — "It weighs heavily on the heavens and the earth. It will not overtake you but all of a sudden" (7:187). And He said: إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ أَكادُ أُخْفِيها لِتُجْزى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما تَسْعى — "Indeed the Hour is bound to come: I almost conceal it, so that every soul may be rewarded for what it strives for" (20:15). And He said: عَمَّ يَتَساءَلُونَ عَنِ النَّبَإِ الْعَظِيمِ — "What is it that they are questioning each other about? About the Great Tiding" (78:1-2).

His saying, the Exalted: أَيَحْسَبُ الْإِنْسانُ أَلَّنْ نَجْمَعَ عِظامَهُ — "Does man suppose that We shall not put together his bones?" (75:3). Supposition (الحسبان) means thinking or assuming (الظن). Putting the bones together is an allusion (كناية) to restoration to life after death. The interrogative is for rebuke (توبيخ), and the meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلى قادِرِينَ عَلى أَنْ نُسَوِّيَ بَنانَهُ — "Yes indeed, We are able to proportion [even] his fingertips!" (75:4). That is: "Yes, We shall gather them." The word "able" (قادِرِينَ) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the subject of the omitted verb following "Yes" (بلى). The fingertips (البنان) are the ends of the fingers, or it is said: the fingers themselves. Proportioning the fingertips (تسوية البنان) means fashioning them exactly as they were. The meaning is: "Yes, We shall gather them while We are able to fashion his fingertips into the forms they possessed in Our initial creation."

The specific mention of the fingertips (البنان) is perhaps an allusion to the wondrous nature of their creation, given their forms and the particularities of their structure and number. From these arise immense benefits that can hardly be counted—various types of grasping, extending, taking, giving, and all other subtle movements, delicate tasks, and fine crafts by which man is distinguished from other animals. This is in addition to the shapes and lines upon them, from which secret after secret continues to be revealed to man.

It has also been said that "proportioning the fingertips" means making the fingers of the hands and feet uniform and solid like a single piece, without separation, such as the camel's hoof or the donkey's hoof. The meaning would then be: "We are able to make them a single piece, such that man would then be unable to perform the various crafts he can do with distinct fingers." However, the previous view is more plausible.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ يُرِيدُ الْإِنْسانُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمامَهُ — "Rather man desires to go on living viciously" (75:5). Al-Raghib said: "Cleaving (الفجر) is to split something with a wide split." He said: "Viciousness (الفجور) is the splitting of the veil of religiosity (ديانة); it is said: 'he acted viciously' (فجر فجورا), so he is a 'profligate' (فاجر), the plural of which is fujjar (فجار) and fajarah (فجرة)." End quote. "Before him" (أمام) is a spatial adverb (ظرف مكان) used metaphorically for the future (مستقبل الزمان). What is meant by his "viciousness before him" is his profligacy throughout the remainder of his life as long as he lives. The pronoun in "before him" (أَمامَهُ) refers to man.

His saying: "to go on living viciously" (لِيَفْجُرَ أَمامَهُ) is a causal explanation (تعليل) that stands in place of what it explains—which is the denial of the Resurrection and the restoration to life after death. The word "rather" (بَلْ) marks a transition (إضراب) from his mere supposition that there is no Resurrection.

The meaning is: He does not merely suppose that We shall not gather his bones; rather, he desires to deny the Resurrection so that he may act viciously for the rest of his life, since there would be no motive for faith and godwariness (تقوى) if there were no Resurrection for reckoning and recompense.

This is what the context provides regarding the meaning of the verse. Others have mentioned different interpretations for the verse that are far-fetched and do not harmonize with the context, so we have refrained from mentioning them.

The mention of "man" (الإنسان) in the verse is an instance of using a manifest noun (الظاهر) in place of a pronoun (الضمير). The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to increase the rebuke and intensify the reprimand. This is repeated in this verse and the three following verses four times.

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الْقِيامَةِ — "He asks, 'When will be the Day of Resurrection?'" (75:6). It appears that this is an elaboration of His saying: "Rather man desires to go on living viciously," thereby providing the reason. It indicates that the questioner is in a state of denial, and the question is one of mockery. For it is obligatory upon one who is called to faith and godwariness, and warned of this Great Tiding—with the presence of clear signs and the establishment of definitive proofs—to take heed, equip himself with faith and godwariness, and prepare for the meeting of that Day, whether it be near or far. For everything that is coming is near. He should not ask: "When will the Hour occur?" or "When is the Day of Resurrection?" Such is only the question of a mocking denier.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا بَرِقَ الْبَصَرُ وَخَسَفَ الْقَمَرُ وَجُمِعَ الشَّمْسُ وَالْقَمَرُ — "But when the sight is dazed, and the moon is eclipsed, and the sun and the moon are brought together" (75:7-9). He mentions a number of the portents of the Hour. The dazing (بريق) of the sight is its confusion in vision and its bewilderment. The eclipse (خسوف) of the moon is the disappearance of its light.

His saying, the Exalted: يَقُولُ الْإِنْسانُ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَيْنَ الْمَفَرُّ — "On that day man will say, 'Where is the escape?'" (75:10). That is: where is the place of flight? His saying "Where is the escape?"—despite the manifestation of Divine Sovereignty to him and his knowledge that there is no place of escape or flight on that day—is due to the manifestation of his ingrained habits (ملكات) on that day. In the world, he used to ask for an escape whenever he fell into hardship or was threatened by destruction. This is similar to their denial of polytheism (الشرك) on that day and their swearing of lies. The Exalted said: ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَاللهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ — "Then their only excuse will be to say, 'By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists'" (6:23). And He said: يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَحْلِفُونَ لَهُ كَما يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ — "The day Allah will raise them all together, they will swear to Him as they swear to you" (58:18).

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا لا وَزَرَ — "No indeed! There is no refuge!" (75:11). This is a deterrent (ردع) against their seeking an escape. A refuge (الوزر) is a place of shelter, such as a mountain, a fortress, or otherwise. This is the speech of the Exalted, not a continuation of man's speech.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلى رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْمُسْتَقَرُّ — "With your Lord is the [final] abode on that day" (75:12). The address is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The advancement of "With your Lord" (إِلى رَبِّكَ), which is connected to "the abode" (الْمُسْتَقَرُّ), signifies exclusivity (حصر). Thus, there is no abode with anyone else; there is no refuge or shelter to which one can flee for protection.

This is because man is journeying toward Him, the Exalted, as He said: يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً فَمُلاقِيهِ — "O man! You are laboring toward your Lord laboriously, and you will encounter Him" (84:6). And He said: إِنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الرُّجْعى — "Indeed the return is to your Lord" (96:8). And He said: وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى — "And that the ultimate term is with your Lord" (53:42). Thus, he is an encounterer of his Lord, returning and ending with Him; no veil shall hide him from Him, and no barrier shall prevent him from Him. As for the veil referred to in His saying: كَلَّا بَلْ رانَ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَنْ رَبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَمَحْجُوبُونَ — "No indeed! Rather their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning. No indeed! They will be barred from [the mercy of] their Lord on that day" (83:14-15), the context of the two verses indicates that what is meant is the veil of deprivation from honor, not the veil of ignorance or absence.

It is also possible that the meaning of his abode being "with Him" is that the final outcome of where he settles—whether in felicity or wretchedness, Paradise or Fire—returns to His will, the Exalted. Whomever He wills, He places in Paradise, and they are the God-conscious (المتقون); and whomever He wills, He places in the Fire, and they are the guilty (المجرمون). The Exalted said: يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ — "He punishes whom He wishes, and forgives whom He wishes" (5:40).

It could also mean that their stability on that day is subject to His judgment, the Exalted; for it is His judgment that is effective over them and none other. The Exalted said: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ لَهُ الْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ — "Everything is to perish except His Face. All judgement belongs to Him, and to Him you will be brought back" (28:88).

His saying, the Exalted: يُنَبَّؤُا الْإِنْسانُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بِما قَدَّمَ وَأَخَّرَ — "That day man will be informed of what he has sent ahead and left behind" (75:13). What is meant by "what he has sent ahead and left behind" is the good or evil deeds he performed at the beginning of his life and at its end; or the good or evil deeds he performed before his death, and the good or evil traditions (سنة) he established that remained after him. Thus, he is rewarded for the good deeds and punished for the evil ones.

It has also been said: what is meant by "what he sent ahead" is the good or evil he performed, for which he is rewarded or punished; and by "what he left behind" is the good or evil he omitted, for which he is punished or rewarded. It has also been said: what he sent ahead of acts of disobedience and what he delayed of acts of obedience. It has also been said: what he sent ahead of obedience to Allah and what he delayed of His rights and thus neglected. It has also been said: what he sent ahead of his wealth for himself and what he left for his heirs. These are weak views, far-fetched from the understanding.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ وَلَوْ أَلْقى مَعاذِيرَهُ — "Rather man is a witness against himself, though he should offer his excuses" (75:14-15). This is a transition (إضراب) from His saying: "man will be informed...", etc. Insight (البصيرة) is the vision of the heart and internal perception (الإدراك الباطني). Its attribution to man is either for emphasis—like saying "Zayd is justice itself"—or the implied meaning is that man possesses insight over himself.

It has also been said that "insight" (البصيرة) means proof (الحجة), as in His saying: ما أَنْزَلَ هؤُلاءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بَصائِرَ — "No one has sent these down except the Lord of the heavens and the earth as eye-openers" (17:102). Thus, man himself is a proof against himself on that day, when he is questioned about his hearing, his sight, and his heart, and when his hearing, his sight, and his skin bear witness against him, and his hands and feet speak. The Exalted said:

إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَالْبَصَرَ وَالْفُؤادَ كُلُّ أُولئِكَ كانَ عَنْهُ مَسْؤُلاً — "Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable" (17:36). And He said: شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَأَبْصارُهُمْ وَجُلُودُهُمْ — "their hearing, their eyes and their skins will bear witness against them" (41:20). And He said: وَتُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ — "their hands will speak to Us, and their feet will bear witness" (36:65).

His saying: وَلَوْ أَلْقى مَعاذِيرَهُ — "though he should offer his excuses." Excuses (المعاذير) is the plural of ma'dhirah (معذرة), which is the mention of obstacles that prevent a required action. The meaning is: he possesses insight over himself even if he were to argue for himself and offer excuses to divert the punishment from it.

It has also been said: ma'adhir (معاذير) is the plural of mi'dhar (معذار), which means a veil or curtain (الستر). The meaning would then be: even if he were to let down the veils to hide what he has done, his own self is a witness against him. The ultimate outcome of both interpretations is the same.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: وَلا أُقْسِمُ بِالنَّفْسِ اللَّوَّامَةِ, he said: "The soul of Adam which disobeyed, so Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, reproached it."

I say: Its correspondence to the verse is obscure.

In the same source, regarding His saying: بَلْ يُرِيدُ الْإِنْسانُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمامَهُ, he said: "He puts the sin forward and delays repentance, saying: 'I shall repent later.'"

In the same source, regarding His saying: فَإِذا بَرِقَ الْبَصَرُ, he said: "The sight is dazed such that he cannot blink."

In the same source, regarding His saying: بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ ـ وَلَوْ أَلْقى مَعاذِيرَهُ, he said: "He knows what he has done even if he offers excuses."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to 'Umar ibn Yazid, who said: "I was having dinner with Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) and he recited this verse: بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ وَلَوْ أَلْقى مَعاذِيرَهُ. Then he said: 'O Abu Hafs, what does a man gain by offering excuses to people contrary to what Allah knows of him? The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say: "Whoever conceals a secret, Allah will clothe him in its cloak—if it is good, then good; and if it is evil, then evil."'"

In al-Majma', al-'Ayyashi narrated with his chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "What does one of you gain by displaying good and concealing evil? Is it not the case that when he returns to himself, he knows it is not so? And Allah, the Glorified, says: بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ. Indeed, when the secret (السريرة) is rectified, the manifest (العلانية) becomes strong."

I say: It is also narrated in Usul al-Kafi with its chain to Fadl Abu al-'Abbas from him (peace be upon him).

In the same source, from al-'Ayyashi from Zurarah, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the limit of illness that allows its sufferer to break the fast. He said: بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ — 'He knows best what he can endure.'"

I say: It is also narrated in al-Faqih.

Ayahs

لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِۦ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِۦٓ (١٦)
Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it. (16)
إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُۥ وَقُرْءَانَهُۥ (١٧)
Indeed it is up to Us to put it together and to recite it. (17)
فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَـٰهُ فَٱتَّبِعْ قُرْءَانَهُۥ (١٨)
And when We have recited it, follow its recitation. (18)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَهُۥ (١٩)
Then, its exposition [also] lies with Us. (19)
كَلَّا بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ (٢٠)
No! Indeed, you love this transitory life (20)
وَتَذَرُونَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ (٢١)
and forsake the Hereafter. (21)
وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ (٢٢)
Some faces will be fresh on that day, (22)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ (٢٣)
looking to their Lord, (23)
وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بَاسِرَةٌ (٢٤)
and some faces will be scowling on that day, (24)
تَظُنُّ أَن يُفْعَلَ بِهَا فَاقِرَةٌ (٢٥)
knowing that they will be dealt out a punishment breaking the spine. (25)
كَلَّآ إِذَا بَلَغَتِ ٱلتَّرَاقِىَ (٢٦)
No indeed! When it reaches up to the collar bones (26)
وَقِيلَ مَنْ ۜ رَاقٍ (٢٧)
and it is said, ‘Who will take him up?’ (27)
وَظَنَّ أَنَّهُ ٱلْفِرَاقُ (٢٨)
and he knows that it is the [time of] parting, (28)
وَٱلْتَفَّتِ ٱلسَّاقُ بِٱلسَّاقِ (٢٩)
and each shank clasps the other shank, (29)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمَسَاقُ (٣٠)
that day he shall be driven toward your Lord. (30)
فَلَا صَدَّقَ وَلَا صَلَّىٰ (٣١)
He neither confirmed [the messages of Allah], nor did he pray, (31)
وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (٣٢)
but denied [them] and turned away, (32)
ثُمَّ ذَهَبَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ يَتَمَطَّىٰٓ (٣٣)
and went back swaggering to his family. (33)
أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰ (٣٤)
So, woe to you! Woe to you! (34)
ثُمَّ أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰٓ (٣٥)
Again, woe to you! Woe to you! (35)
أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَن يُتْرَكَ سُدًى (٣٦)
Does man suppose that he has been abandoned to futility? (36)
أَلَمْ يَكُ نُطْفَةً مِّن مَّنِىٍّ يُمْنَىٰ (٣٧)
Was he not a drop of emitted semen, (37)
ثُمَّ كَانَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ (٣٨)
and then a clinging mass? Whereat He created and proportioned [him], (38)
فَجَعَلَ مِنْهُ ٱلزَّوْجَيْنِ ٱلذَّكَرَ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰٓ (٣٩)
and made of him the two sexes, male and female. (39)
أَلَيْسَ ذَٰلِكَ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُحْـِۧىَ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ (٤٠)
Is not [someone like] that able to revive the dead? (40)

(Commentary)

This section completes the description of the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) by considering the state of people therein and their division into two groups: one with radiant faces, rejoicing, and another with scowling faces, frowning and despairing of salvation. It points out that this trajectory begins from the moment death descends. Furthermore, it indicates that man is not left aimless (سدى); for He who created him initially is capable of bringing him to life a second time. With this, the Surah concludes.

His saying, the Exalted: «لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ» (Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it) until His saying: «ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَهُ» (Then it is indeed for Us to explain it). What the context (سياق) of these four verses provides—along with the preceding and succeeding verses describing the Day of Resurrection—is that they are parenthetical (معترضة), containing a divine etiquette (أدب إلهي) that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded to observe when receiving the revelation (الوحي) of the Noble Quran. He was not to rush into reciting what had not yet been recited to him, nor move his tongue with it, but rather to listen until the revelation was completed.

Thus, these four verses carry the same meaning as His saying, the Exalted: "and do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation is completed to you" (20:114).

The discourse in these verses follows the manner of a speaker among us during a conversation with an interlocutor, when the latter rushes to complete some of the speaker's words with a word or two before the speaker utters them. This distracts the listener from pure listening (الإنصات). Consequently, the speaker interrupts his discourse, interjecting to say: "Do not hasten with my words; listen so that you may understand what I am saying to you," and then continues his discourse.

Thus, His saying: «لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ» (Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it)—the address here is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the two pronouns refer to the Quran being revealed or to the revelation (الوحي). The meaning is: Do not move your tongue with the revelation to receive it hastily, thereby preceding Us in reciting what We have not yet recited. This is, as mentioned, similar in meaning to His saying: "and do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation is completed to you" (20:114).

And His saying: «إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُ وَقُرْآنَهُ» (Indeed, it is for Us to assemble it and its recitation). The word "recitation" (القرآن) here is a verbal noun (مصدر) like "the Criterion" (الفرقان) and "preponderance" (الرجحان). The two pronouns refer to the revelation. The meaning is: Do not hasten with it, for it is Our responsibility to assemble what We reveal to you by joining its parts together and reciting it to you. Nothing of it shall escape Us such that you would need to precede Us in reciting what We have not yet revealed.

It has been said that the meaning is: It is for Us to assemble it in your chest such that nothing of its meanings escapes you, and to establish its recitation upon your tongue so that you may recite it whenever you wish. However, this is not without some far-fetchedness (بعد).

And His saying: «فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَاهُ فَاتَّبِعْ قُرْآنَهُ» (So when We have recited it, follow its recitation). That is, when We have completed its recitation to you as revelation, then follow Our recitation of it and recite after its completion.

It has been said that "following its recitation" means following it mentally through silent listening (الإنصات) and complete focus (التوجه التام) toward it; this is an acceptable meaning.

It has also been said: Follow its recitation in commands and prohibitions. Others said: Follow its recitation by repetition until it is firmly established in the mind. Both are far-fetched meanings.

And His saying: «ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَهُ» (Then it is indeed for Us to explain it). That is, it is for Us to clarify it to you after it was Our responsibility to assemble and recite it. The word "then" (ثم) indicates a rank-based delay (التأخير الرتبي), because explanation (البيان) is ranked after assembly and recitation.

It has been said the meaning is: Then it is for Us to explain it to the people through your tongue, preserving it in your mind from change or loss until you recite it to the people.

Some have said regarding the meaning of these verses that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to move his tongue during revelation with what was being delivered to him of the Quran out of fear that he might forget it. Thus, he was forbidden from that by these verses and commanded to listen until the revelation was complete. Therefore, the pronoun in "Do not move [your tongue] with it" refers to the Quran or the revelation in terms of what had already been recited to him, not what had not yet been recited.

However, this does not perfectly suit the context (سياق) of the verses, given the prohibition of haste (العجل) and the command to follow His recitation after it is read. Likewise, His saying, "Indeed, it is for Us to assemble it and its recitation"—all of this is more apparent in the meaning previously mentioned.

Some have suggested regarding the meaning of these verses—a view they chose—that it does not refer to the Quran at all, but rather to the servants' reading of their records on the Day of Resurrection. They argue that the preceding and succeeding verses indicate this, and there is nothing in it to prove it is the Quran or related to the rulings of this world.

According to this view, it is a rebuke and reprimand to the person when haste does not benefit him. It says: Do not move your tongue with what you read from your scroll containing your deeds. That is: Read your book and do not hasten; for when this person, who is a witness against himself, sees his evil deeds, he becomes distressed and hastens. So it is said to him as a rebuke: Do not hasten, and be steady so that you may know the proof against you.

"For We shall assemble it for you; so when We have assembled it, follow what has been assembled against you by submitting to its judgment and surrendering to its consequences, for you cannot deny it. Then it is for Us to explain it if you were to deny." [End of quote].

This is refuted by the fact that a parenthetical statement (المعترضة) does not require evidence from what precedes or follows it for its meaning to be complete. Furthermore, the similarity of His saying: "and do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation is completed to you" in its context to these verses supports their similarity in meaning.

Others have said that the four verses are connected to the preceding discourse about the Day of Resurrection. The address "Do not move" is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the pronoun "with it" refers to the Day of Resurrection. The meaning would be: Do not utter questions about the timing of the Resurrection at all, even if you are not a denier or a mocker, "to hasten it"—meaning to hasten knowledge of it. "Indeed, it is for Us to assemble it and its recitation"—meaning it is necessary by Wisdom that We assemble those whom We shall assemble therein and reveal the explanation of its description to you in the Quran. "So when We have recited it, follow its recitation"—meaning when We recite what pertains to it, follow that by acting upon what it necessitates of preparation for it. "Then it is for Us to explain it"—meaning to manifest that by the blowing of the Trumpet. [End of summary]. This view is as you see [weak].

It was previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying: "and do not hasten with the Quran" that this prohibition of hastening with the Quran supports what has been narrated in traditions: that the Quran has a singular, instantaneous descent (نزول) upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله distinct from its gradual descent.

His saying, the Exalted: «كَلَّا بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ الْعَاجِلَةَ وَتَذَرُونَ الْآخِرَةَ» No! Indeed you love the transitory life, and leave aside the Hereafter. (75:20-21). This is an address to the people and is not a generalization of the previous address at all, because the address "Do not move" is parenthetical and unrelated to either side of it. His saying: "No" (كلا) is a deterrent (ردع) against the previous statement: "Does man suppose that We shall not put together his bones?" (75:3). His saying: "Indeed you love the transitory life"—meaning the immediate life, which is the life of this world—"and leave aside the Hereafter"—meaning you abandon the life of the Hereafter. The transition (الإضراب) in the speech is a transition from the supposition of not being brought back to life after death, similar to the transition in His saying: "Rather man desires to go on living viciously." (75:5).

His saying, the Exalted: «وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَاضِرَةٌ إِلَى رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ» Some faces on that day will be radiant, looking at their Lord. (75:22-23). This is a description of the Day of Resurrection by the division of faces therein into two types: radiant and scowling. Radiance (النضرة) of the face, color, or trees refers to their beauty and splendor.

The meaning—considering what stands in contrast to it in His saying: "And some faces on that day will be scowling"—is that on the day the Resurrection occurs, some faces will be beautiful, beaming, showing joy and cheerfulness. The Exalted said: "You will perceive in their faces the freshness of bliss" (83:24), and He said: "and granted them freshness and joy" (76:11).

His saying: "looking at their Lord" (إِلَى رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ) is a second predicate for "faces." The phrase "at their Lord" (إِلَى رَبِّهَا) is connected to "looking" (ناظرة) and is placed before it to signify exclusivity (الحصر) or importance.

What is meant by "looking" at Him, the Exalted, is not sensory vision (النظر الحسي) pertaining to the physical, material eye—which definitive proofs have established as impossible regarding Him, the Exalted. Rather, what is meant is the vision of the heart (النظر القلبي) and the seeing of the heart through the reality of faith (حقيقة الإيمان), as necessitated by rational proof and indicated by the narrated reports from the People of Infallibility (أهل العصمة) عليه‌السلام. We have cited a portion of these under the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: "He said, ‘My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You.’" (7:143), and His saying, the Exalted: "The heart did not deny what it saw." (53:11).

Thus, the hearts of these people are directed toward their Lord; no distractor among the causes (الأسباب) diverts them from Him, the Glorified, for all causes are severed on that day. They do not stand at any station of that Day, nor cross any of its stages, except that divine mercy encompasses them—"and they will be secure from the terror of that day" (27:89). They do not witness any scene of Paradise, nor enjoy any of its bliss, except that they witness their Lord through it. This is because they do not look at anything, nor see anything, except insofar as it is a sign (آية) of Allah, the Glorified. Looking at a sign insofar as it is a sign, and seeing it, is a looking at and seeing the One to whom the sign points (ذي الآية).

From here, the answer becomes clear to the objection raised against the view that placing "at their Lord" before "looking" signifies exclusivity—namely, that it is necessary that they look at other than Him, the Exalted, such as the blessings of Paradise.

The answer is that since they are not veiled from their Lord, their looking at everything they behold is only because it is a sign. A sign, insofar as it is a sign, does not veil the One it points to, nor does it stand between Him and the one looking at Him. Thus, looking at the sign is looking at the One pointed to. Therefore, in reality, these people look at none but their Lord.

As for the response that "at their Lord" was placed first to maintain the rhyme (رعاية الفواصل), or that even if it were for exclusivity, looking at others besides Him is considered as nothing compared to looking at Him, or that looking at Him occurs in some states and not all—these are not free from the artifice of restriction (التكلف) without a restrictor. Furthermore, the "looking" is attributed to the "faces" (الوجوه), not to the "eyes" (العيون) or "sights" (الأبصار), and the faces of the people of Paradise are directed toward their Lord perpetually, without facing anything else.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ بَاسِرَةٌ تَظُنُّ أَنْ يُفْعَلَ بِهَا فَاقِرَةٌ» And some faces on that day will be scowling, knowing that a spine-breaker will be dealt to them. (75:24-25). Scowling (البسور) is explained as intense frowning, and "supposing" (الظن) here means knowing (العلم). "Spine-breaker" (فاقرة) is an adjective whose noun is omitted—i.e., a spine-breaking deed. It is derived from "breaking the vertebrae" (فقار) of the back. It is also said to be from "branding the camel's nose" (فقرت البعير) with fire.

The meaning is: And some faces on that day will be intensely frowning, knowing that a deed will be done to them that breaks their backs or brands their noses with fire. It is also possible that "you suppose" (تظن) is an address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله as a listener, with "suppose" carrying its common meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: «كَلَّا إِذَا بَلَغَتِ التَّرَاقِيَ» No! When it reaches the collarbones (75:26). This is a deterrent (ردع) against their love for the transitory life and their preference for it over the Hereafter. It is as if it were said: Desist from that, for it will not last for you; death will soon descend upon you, and you will be driven to your Lord. The subject of "reaches" (بلغت) is omitted, indicated by the context, as in His saying: "So when it reaches the throat" (56:83). The implied meaning is: When the soul (النفس) reaches the collarbones.

The collarbones (التراقي) are the bones surrounding the pit of the neck on the right and left; the plural of tarquwah. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقِيلَ مَنْ رَاقٍ» and it is said, ‘Who is a healer?’ (75:27). "Healer" (راقٍ) is the active participle of "incantation" (الرقى). That is, those present of his family and friends say: "Who can perform an incantation for him and cure him?"—a word of despair. It is also said the meaning is: Some angels say to others: "Who shall ascend (يرقى) with his soul among the angels? The angels of mercy or the angels of punishment?"

His saying, the Exalted: «وَظَنَّ أَنَّهُ الْفِرَاقُ» and he knows that it is the [time of] parting (75:28). That is, the dying person knows from witnessing these states that it is his departure from the transitory life which he loved and preferred over the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَالْتَفَّتِ السَّاقُ بِالسَّاقِ» and [one] leg twists around the [other] leg (75:29). Its apparent meaning is the twisting of the dying person's leg with his other leg due to the cessation of life flowing in the extremities of the body when the soul reaches the collarbones.

It is said the meaning is the intertwining of the severity of the Hereafter's affair with the affair of this world. It is also said: the intertwining of the state of death with the state of life. It is also said: the intertwining of the "leg" of this world—which is the distress of the agony of death—with the "leg" of the Hereafter—which is the severity of the terror of what is to come.

There is no linguistic evidence for any of these specific meanings. However, it is possible to say that the "twisting of the leg around the leg" refers to the overwhelming of hardships and their succession upon him one after another from that moment until the Day of Resurrection; thus, it would apply to all these meanings.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِلَى رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْمَسَاقُ» on that day the drive will be toward your Lord. (75:30). "The drive" (المساق) is a verbal noun meaning the act of driving (السوق). The meaning of the drive being toward Him, the Exalted, on that day is the return to Him. The term "drive" (المساق) is used to indicate that man has no choice in this journey and no escape from it; he is driven and moved from the day of his death—which is His saying: "on that day the drive will be toward your Lord"—until he arrives before his Lord on the Day of Resurrection—which is His saying: "on that day the station will be with your Lord" (75:12).

If the placement of "toward your Lord" first is for exclusivity, it signifies the restriction of the ultimate end to the return to Him, the Exalted.

It has been said the speech implies an omitted word (مضاف), and the placement of "toward your Lord" first is for exclusivity, meaning: Toward the judgment of your Lord on that day is the drive—i.e., he is driven so that Allah may judge and decree concerning him. Or the implied meaning is: Toward the promised place of your Lord, which is Paradise and Hell. It has also been said the meaning of the drive returning to Him is that He, the Exalted, is the Driver and none else. The correct view is what was mentioned first.

His saying, the Exalted: «فَلَا صَدَّقَ وَلَا صَلَّى وَلَكِنْ كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّى ثُمَّ ذَهَبَ إِلَى أَهْلِهِ يَتَمَطَّى» He neither confirmed [the truth] nor prayed, but he denied and turned away, then he went to his family swaggering. (75:31-33). The pronouns refer to the "man" mentioned in His saying: "Does man suppose..." etc. What is meant by the negated "confirmation" (التصديق) is the confirmation of the true call contained in the Noble Quran. By the negated "prayer" (التصلية) is meant the devotional turning toward Him, the Exalted, through prayer (الصلاة), which is the pillar of religion.

"Swaggering" (التمطي)—according to al-Majma‘—is the stretching of the body out of laziness; its origin is to twist one's mata (back). The meaning of his swaggering in his gait is to strut and walk with conceit—a metaphor.

The meaning is: This man did not confirm the call in its beliefs, nor did he pray to his Lord—meaning he did not follow it in its practical branches, the pillar of which is prayer—but he denied it and turned away from it, then went to his family strutting and walking conceitedly in arrogance.

His saying, the Exalted: «أَوْلَى لَكَ فَأَوْلَى ثُمَّ أَوْلَى لَكَ فَأَوْلَى» Woe to you! [Woe] to you! Again, woe to you! [Woe] to you! (75:34-35). There is no doubt that this is a word of threat (تهديد) repeated to emphasize the threat. It is not unlikely—and Allah knows best—that His saying: "Woe to you" (أَوْلَى لَكَ) is a predicate for an omitted subject, which is a pronoun referring to the mentioned state of this man: that he did not confirm nor pray, but denied and turned away, then went to his family swaggering. Affirming his current state for him is a metonymy (كناية) for affirming its necessary consequence of retribution and punishment.

Thus, the speech—which is a word delivered by Allah, the Exalted, to this man—is a word of sealing (كلمة طبع) with which Allah sealed his heart, depriving him of faith and godwariness (التقوى), and decreeing that he is among the companions of the Fire. These two verses resemble, in a way, His saying, the Exalted: "But when a definitive surah is sent down and fighting is mentioned in it, you see those in whose hearts is a sickness looking at you with the look of one fainted at death. So woe to them!" (48:20).

The meaning is: The state you are in is more fitting (أولى) and likely for you, and again more fitting, then more fitting for you and again more fitting, so that you may taste the evil consequence of your affair and the punishment prepared for you may seize you.

It has been said: Awla laka is an indeclinable verbal noun meaning "May evil follow you after evil."

It has been said: Awla is a past tense verb of supplication from al-wali meaning "closeness." The subject of the verb is a hidden pronoun referring to "destruction," and the lam is extra. The meaning is: "May destruction be close to you."

It has been said: The subject is a hidden pronoun referring to Him, the Exalted, and the lam is extra; the meaning is: "May Allah bring upon you what you dislike." Or it is not extra, meaning: "May Allah bring you close to what you dislike."

It has been said: Its meaning is that "blame" is more fitting (أولى) for you than leaving it, but it was omitted and became so frequent in speech that it took the place of "Woe to you" (الويل لك), becoming one of those omitted elements that may not be manifested.

It has been said: The meaning is: "May Allah destroy you with a destruction closer to you than every evil and destruction."

It has been said: Awla is a superlative (أفعل تفضيل) meaning "more worthy," and it is a predicate for an omitted subject estimated as fits the context. The estimation here is: "The Fire is more fitting (أولى) for you"—i.e., you are more deserving of it and worthy of it, and again more fitting.

These are weak views not free from artifice (تكلف). The last view is close to what we presented but is not quite it.

His saying, the Exalted: «أَيَحْسَبُ الْإِنْسَانُ أَنْ يُتْرَكَ سُدًى» Does man suppose that he will be left to himself, aimless? (75:36). A conclusion in which there is a return to what was at the opening of the Surah in His saying: "Does man suppose that We shall not put together his bones?"

The interrogation is for rebuke (توبيخ). "Aimless" (السدى) means neglected. The meaning is: Does man think he will be left neglected, not cared for, such that he will not be resurrected by being brought back to life after death? The necessary consequence of this would be that he is neither commanded nor recompensed.

His saying, the Exalted: «أَلَمْ يَكُ نُطْفَةً مِنْ مَنِيٍّ يُمْنَى» Was he not a drop of emitted semen? (75:37). The subject of "was" is a pronoun referring to man. The "emission" (إمناء) of semen is its pouring into the womb.

His saying, the Exalted: «ثُمَّ كَانَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقَ فَسَوَّى» Then he was a clinging mass; then He created and fashioned [him] (75:38). That is, then man—or the semen—was a piece of clotted blood, so He proportioned him and shaped him with balance and completion.

His saying, the Exalted: «فَجَعَلَ مِنْهُ الزَّوْجَيْنِ الذَّكَرَ وَالْأُنْثَى» and made of him the two sexes, the male and the female. (75:39). That is, He made from man the two types: male and female.

His saying, the Exalted: «أَلَيْسَ ذَلِكَ بِقَادِرٍ عَلَى أَنْ يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتَى» Is not such a one able to revive the dead? (75:40). An argument for the Resurrection (البعث) which they deny by deeming it improbable, based on the universality of Power and its establishment over the initial creation. Re-creation (الإعادة) does not require more effort than the beginning; rather, it is easier. The discourse on the formulation of this argument has been presented repeatedly in the commentary of the verses addressing it.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Tayalisi, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, ibn Jarir, ibn al-Mundhir, ibn Abi Hatim, ibn al-Anbari in al-Masahif, al-Tabarani, ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym, and al-Bayhaqi together in al-Dala’il narrated from ibn ‘Abbas who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to experience hardship from the revelation (التنزيل). He would move his tongue and lips with it out of fear that it might escape him—meaning he wanted to memorize it. So Allah revealed: «لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ ـ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُ وَقُرْآنَهُ» (Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it—Indeed, it is for Us to assemble it and its recitation). He said: 'It is for Us to assemble it in your chest then We shall recite it.' «فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَاهُ» (So when We have recited it)—He says: 'When We send it down to you'—«فَاتَّبِعْ قُرْآنَهُ» (follow its recitation)—'so listen to it and be silent.' «ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَهُ» (Then it is indeed for Us to explain it)—'explain it with your tongue.' In another wording: 'It is for Us to recite it.' After that, whenever Gabriel came to him, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would lower his head—in another wording: 'would listen'—and when he left, he would recite it as Allah had promised him."

In the same source: ibn al-Mundhir and ibn Mardawayh narrated from ibn ‘Abbas who said: "Whenever the Quran was revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he would hasten with its recitation to memorize it. So this verse was revealed: «لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسَانَكَ» (Do not move your tongue with it)."

And the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would not know the end of a Surah until بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم (In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful) was revealed to him.

I say: What is in the meaning of the beginning of the hadith was narrated in al-Majma‘ from ibn Jubayr, and there are several traditions with this meaning. It was previously mentioned that there is some obscurity in applying this meaning to the verses.

In Tafsir al-Qummi regarding His saying, the Exalted: «كَلَّا بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ الْعَاجِلَةَ» (No! Indeed you love the transitory life)—he said: "The present world"—«وَتَذَرُونَ الْآخِرَةَ» (and leave aside the Hereafter)—he said: "You abandon [it]." «وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَاضِرَةٌ» (Some faces on that day will be radiant)—meaning bright. «إِلَى رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ» (looking at their Lord)—he said: "They look toward the face of Allah, meaning the mercy of Allah and His blessing."

In al-‘Uyun, in the chapter on what has been narrated from Imam al-Rida عليه‌السلام regarding reports of monotheism (التوحيد), with its chain to Ibrahim ibn Abi Mahmud, who said: "Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه‌السلام said regarding His saying, the Exalted: «وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَاضِرَةٌ إِلَى رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ» (Some faces on that day will be radiant, looking at their Lord)—it means bright, awaiting (تنتظر) the reward of their Lord."

I say: It was narrated in al-Tawhid, al-Ihtijaj, and al-Majma‘ from Imam Ali عليه‌السلام. An objection was raised against taking "looking" (ناظرة) to mean "awaiting" (منتظرة) because "awaiting" (الانتظار) is not made transitive with the preposition "to" (إلى), but is transitive by itself. This was refuted in Majma‘ al-Bayan by citing the words of Jamil ibn Ma‘mar:

And when I look toward you (إليك) from a king, And the sea is before you, you have granted me blessings.

And the words of another:

I am indeed looking toward (إليك) what you promised us, The look of the poor man toward the wealthy and affluent.

In al-Kashshaf, the use of "looking" in the verse to mean "awaiting" was considered a metonymical usage (استعمالا كنائيا), which is a fine meaning.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: ibn Abi Shaybah, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi, ibn Jarir, ibn al-Mundhir, al-Ajurri in al-Shari‘ah, al-Daraqutni in al-Ru’yah, al-Hakim, ibn Mardawayh, al-Lalika’i in al-Sunnah, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from ibn ‘Umar who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Indeed, the one with the lowest station among the people of Paradise is he who looks at his gardens, his wives, his bliss, his servants, and his couches for a distance of a thousand years. And the most honored of them before Allah is he who looks at His face morning and evening.'"

Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited: «وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَاضِرَةٌ» (Some faces on that day will be radiant)—he said: "Whiteness and purity"—«إِلَى رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ» (looking at their Lord)—he said: "He looks every day at His face."

I say: The narration is capable of being applied to the meaning we provided in the commentary of the verse. Even setting that aside, it can be interpreted as His mercy, grace, generosity, and other active attributes (صفاته الفعلية). For the "face" (وجه) of a thing is that by which it confronts another, and that by which Allah, the Glorified, confronts His creation is His noble attributes. Thus, looking at the mercy of Allah, His grace, His generosity, and His noble attributes is looking at the Noble Face of Allah.

In the same source: ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas ibn Malik who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said regarding the saying of Allah: «وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَاضِرَةٌ إِلَى رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ» (Some faces on that day will be radiant, looking at their Lord)—he said: 'They look at their Lord without modality (بلا كيفية), without a defined limit (حد محدود), and without a known description (صفة معلومة).'"

I say: The narration supports what we presented in the commentary of the verse: that what is meant is the vision of the heart (النظر القلبي) and the seeing of the heart, not the sensory eye. It explains what has been narrated in several traditions from Sunni sources whose apparent meaning suggests anthropomorphism (التشبيه) and that the vision is with the sensory eye, which cannot be separated from limitation.

In Tafsir al-Qummi regarding His saying, the Exalted: «كَلَّا إِذَا بَلَغَتِ التَّرَاقِيَ» (No! When it reaches the collarbones)—he said: "Meaning the soul when it reaches the collarbone." «وَقِيلَ مَنْ رَاقٍ» (and it is said, ‘Who is a healer?’)—he said: "It is said to him: Who can heal you?" «وَظَنَّ أَنَّهُ الْفِرَاقُ» (and he knows that it is the [time of] parting)—he knew it was the parting.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Jabir from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: «وَقِيلَ مَنْ رَاقٍ وَظَنَّ أَنَّهُ الْفِرَاقُ» (and it is said, ‘Who is a healer?’ and he knows that it is the [time of] parting). He said: 'That is the son of Adam when death descends upon him—he says: Is there any doctor? «وَظَنَّ أَنَّهُ الْفِرَاقُ» (and he knows that it is the [time of] parting)—he becomes certain of parting from loved ones.' «وَالْتَفَّتِ السَّاقُ بِالسَّاقِ» (and [one] leg twists around the [other] leg)—he said: 'The world twists with the Hereafter.' «إِلَى رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْمَسَاقُ» (on that day the drive will be toward your Lord)—he said: 'The journey is toward the Lord of all the worlds.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi: «وَالْتَفَّتِ السَّاقُ بِالسَّاقِ» (and [one] leg twists around the [other] leg)—he said: "The world twists with the Hereafter." «إِلَى رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْمَسَاقُ» (on that day the drive will be toward your Lord)—he said: "They are driven toward Allah."

In al-‘Uyun, with its chain from ‘Abd al-‘Azim al-Hasani, who said: "I asked Muhammad ibn Ali al-Rida عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: «أَوْلَى لَكَ فَأَوْلَى ثُمَّ أَوْلَى لَكَ فَأَوْلَى» (Woe to you! [Woe] to you! Again, woe to you! [Woe] to you!). He said: 'Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: Far be you from the good of this world, and far be you from the good of the Hereafter.'"

I say: This can be reconciled with the meaning we provided for the two verses, as well as with some of what has been said regarding them.

In al-Majma‘: A narration states that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله took Abu Jahl by the hand and said to him: "Woe to you! [Woe] to you! Again, woe to you! [Woe] to you!" Abu Jahl replied: "With what do you threaten me? Neither you nor your Lord can do anything to me, and I am indeed the most powerful of the people of this valley." So Allah, the Glorified, revealed exactly what the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had said to him.

I say: Something similar in meaning was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several sources from Qatadah, who said: "It was mentioned to us..." and he cited the hadith.

In Tafsir al-Qummi regarding His saying, the Exalted: «أَيَحْسَبُ الْإِنْسَانُ أَنْ يُتْرَكَ سُدًى» (Does man suppose that he will be left to himself, aimless?)—he said: "Neither held to account, nor punished, nor asked about anything."

In al-‘Ilal, with its chain to Mas‘adah ibn Ziyad, who said: "A man said to Ja‘far ibn Muhammad عليه‌السلام: 'O Abu ‘Abdillah, we were created for wonder (العجب)!' He said: 'And what is that, may Allah be with you?' He said: 'We were created for annihilation (الفناء)!' He replied: 'O nephew, we were created for subsistence (البقاء). How can that which is a Paradise that never perishes and a Fire that is never extinguished be annihilated? Rather, say: We only move from one abode to another.'"

In al-Majma‘: It was narrated in a hadith from al-Bara’ ibn ‘Azib who said: "When this verse was revealed: «أَلَيْسَ ذَلِكَ بِقَادِرٍ عَلَى أَنْ يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتَى» (Is not such a one able to revive the dead?), the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Glory be to You, O Allah, and yes indeed (سبحانك اللهم وبلى).'" This was also narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام.

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abu Hurayrah and others that whenever he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited the verse, he would say: "Glory be to You, O Allah, and yes indeed." Likewise, in al-‘Uyun from Imam al-Rida عليه‌السلام: Whenever he recited the Surah, he would say upon finishing: "Glory be to You, O Allah, yes indeed."
